MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT / Notice
Off Calendar Sep-11-2014 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT is Off calendar. Duplicative entry.=(302/EHG)

AMENDED MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Motion For Summary Judgment Filed By City And County Of San
Off Calendar Sep-11-2014 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES - ATTY. LEE HEPNER & ATTY. WHITNEY LEIGH APPEARING FOR THE PLAINTIFF;
ATTY. ROBB W. KAPLA, DEPUTY CITY ATTORNEY, TEL. NO. 554-4647 APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANT CCSF.

RULING - THE COURT GAVE THE PLAINTIFF THE OPPORTUNITY TO ARGUE THE MATTER DESPITE NOTICE WAS NOT GIVEN AFTER THE TENTATIVE RULING CAME OUT. The Court adopted its tentative ruling. DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO's AMENDED MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT is
Granted. The plain language of CCP sec. 526a explicitly provides that a tax must be assessed against an individual in order for him or her to have taxpayer standing. Defendants met their initial burden by providing evidence showing that a San Francisco tax was never assessed against Plaintiff. (Defendants' Undisputed Material Facts 1-9.) Plaintiff has not raised a triable issue of material fact as to whether a San Francisco tax was assessed against him. Plaintiff cites no cases that establish that payment of a "pass through" tax conveys taxpayer standing. (See Cornelius v. Los Angeles County (1996) 49 Cal.App.4th 1761, 1777-78, rejecting the plaintiff's contention that payment of sales tax confers taxpayer standing because "[s]ales taxes are generally construed to be taxes on the retailer, not the consumer to whom the retailer passes the burden;" Torres v. City of Yorba Linda (1993) 13 Cal.App.4th 1035, 1048, holding that an increase in sales tax paid by customers because of an increase in tax on the retailers does not establish taxpayer standing because "the tax was imposed on the person who sold the goods.") In any event, even if payment of a "pass through" tax did convey standing, Plaintiff submits no admissible evidence establishing that he ever paid a "pass through" tax. In his declaration, Plaintiff states that he "believe[s]" that his rent was increased because his landlord was passing through an increase in property tax. (Declaration of Peter Turner, par. 8.) "Evidence that gives rise to no more than mere speculation cannot be regarded as substantial, and is insufficient to establish a triable issue of material fact." (Sangster v. Paetkau (1998) 68 Cal.App.4th 151, 163.) Further, the Equal Protection argument as to CCP sec. 526a was recently considered and rejected in Wheatherford v. City of San Rafael (2014) 226 Cal.App.4th 460. The court held that the statute is subject to the rational basis test, requiring a plaintiff to show that it serves "no conceivable state purpose." (Id. at 469.) "Courts have noted that it is not irrational to limit standing in taxpayer lawsuits'We also see a rational purpose in limiting taxpayer standing to persons who pay property tax in the jurisdiction corresponding to the public entity defendant. Individuals who have directly paid a tax to the government have obtained a sufficiently personal interest in the illegal expenditure of funds by county officials to become dedicated adversaries." (Id.) ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. Judge: Ernest H. Goldsmith; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Ann Delgado, CSR # 11185 =302/eg
